Figure 3. 3D reconstruction results on T&T [25] and ETH3D [44]. Points with confidence above the 90% quantile are visualised. We compare the error maps of the reconstruction before and after our refinement. In the zoomed-in regions, the ground truth (colored by input RGBs) is overlaid with the predictions (colored by error) to highlight how our method corrects the misalignment of input points.

Figure 11. More Visual Results on MV-dVRK [47]. We compare the error maps of the baselines with those of our refinement and report the AUC@5 mm (%) for each scene. Points with predicted confidence below the 10% quantile are discarded.

Figure 12. More Visual Results on 4D-DRESS [58]. We compare the error maps of the baselines with those of our refinement and report the AUC@5 cm (%) for each scene. Points with predicted confidence below the 10% quantile are discarded.

Table 8. Performance of GGPT across Varying SfM Input Densities. We vary the SfM configuration, including the matcher type, DLT threshold ϵDLT, and reprojection threshold ϵreproj, to obtain SfM point clouds of different coverage (%). We report AUC@5/10 cm on

Figure 4. Comparison between SfMs on ETH3D [44]. Across 4/8/16-view setups, our SfM pipeline achieves consistently better camera pose accuracy, points accuracy, and good points completeness, while retaining the shortest running time.
Table 2. Multi-view 3D reconstruction on out-of-domain datasets. AUC@1/5 cm (% ↑) for human body reconstruction (4D-DRESS [58]) and AUC@1/5 mm (% ↑) for surgical scene reconstruction (MV-dVRK [47]).

Figure 8. Visual Results on ETH3D [44]. We present error maps for all baselines alongside our refined predictions and report the AUC@10 cm (%) for each scene. Points with confidence below the 10 % quantile are discarded. Because the laser ground truth is incomplete, some pixels in the error maps have no valid values.
Table 7. Ablation Studies on GGPT. We report AUC@5/10 cm

Figure 9. Visual Results on ScanNet++ [63]. We compare the error maps between baselines and our refinement and report the AUC@10 cm (%) for each scene. We filter points with predicted confidence below the 10% quantile. On the within-domain ScanNet++, Pi3 [59] achieves saturated performance as it is trained on a large scale of similar indoor scenes, leaving limited room for further improvement. Nevertheless, our method still enhances its performance on other datasets, as demonstrated in the accompanying figures.

Table 6. Ablation of SfM Components on ETH3D. We evaluate design variants across three stages of our SfM—matching, bundle adjustment, and triangulation—and highlight the configuration with the best efficiency or accuracy. For each variant, we report: (1) Time denotes the running time (s ↓) of each stage. (2) Cam. denotes the AUC@1/5◦ (% ↑) for camera-pose estimation. (3) Pts. (% ↑) denotes the Accuracy/Completeness@1 cm (% ↑) of reconstructed points.

Figure 5. Examples on out-of-domain 4D-DRESS [58] with visualisation (top row), and MV-dVRK [47] with both visualisation and reconstruction error (bottom row), predicted by feed-forward networks and enhanced by our method. Our GGPT can significantly improve the multi-view consistency in the reconstruction result.

Figure 2. Overview of our method. We utilise off-the-shelf feed-forward multi-view transformers [22, 54] and dense matchers [10, 69] to predict dense point maps {Pi ∈ RH×W×3}Ni=1, camera parameters {gi}Ni=1, and multi-view correspondences T ∈ RN×N×H×W×2. These correspondences are used for sparse bundle adjustment and multi-view DLT triangulation, producing a geometrically consistent yet incomplete sparse cloud Xs via a lightweight alternative to standard SfM pipelines (Section 3.1). The dense yet multi-view inconsistent point cloud Xd, obtained by combining the predicted per-view point maps, exhibits local misalignments (red boxes) that are then refined under the geometric guidance of Xs by our Geometry-Grounded Point Transformer (GGPT) (Section 3.2), a modified point transformer [61] equipped with specialised geometric embeddings PE(xd→s) and ∆d→s, which encode spatial relations between dense and sparse points to provide stable geometric guidance. The final output is a refined, globally aligned, and geometry-consistent dense point cloud X̂d.

Table 9. Comparison of GPU Memory Consumption and Inference Speed. (a) We show the per-stage GPU memory and running time of our pipeline. (b) We compare our method with feed-forward method [54] and hybrid methods [9, 13] across varying view counts.

Figure 7. Performance vs. number of views on ETH3D. Using the latest dense matcher RoMa v2 [11], our method consistently surpasses the baselines with a favourable runtime profile across a wide range of input views, scaling effectively up to 50.

Table 4. Multi-view 3D reconstruction on out-of-domain datasets. AUC@1/5 cm (% ↑) for human body reconstruction (4D-DRESS [58]) and AUC@1/5 mm (% ↑) for surgical scene reconstruction (MV-dVRK [47]). Here we report additional results with latest dense matcher RoMa v2 [11], which further boosts the performance.

Figure 10. More Visual Results on T&T [44]. We compare the error maps of the baselines with those of our refinement and report the AUC@10 cm (%) for each scene. Points with predicted confidence below the 10% quantile are removed. As the laser ground truth does not cover all pixels, some regions in the error maps remain unlabelled.

Figure 6. GGPT’s Robustness to Sparse Guidance. Test examples from ETH3D [44] (1st row), ScanNet++ [63] (2nd and 3rd rows), and 4D-DRESS [58] (4th row). Points with predicted confidence below the 10% quantile are filtered. SfM points are produced with matchings from RoMa [10]. We visualise the SfM points and compare error maps before and after GGPT refinement. In the zoomedin regions, the ground truth (coloured by input RGBs) is overlaid with the predictions (coloured by error) to highlight how our method corrects misaligned input points. SfM guidance provides little support in textureless regions, such as white walls or black cloth, but GGPT effectively propagates sparse guidance to unguided areas, improving the overall accuracy of the point map. Quantitative evaluation can be found in Tab. 8.
